name: Monster Slash
tagline: Swipe to slash · chain combos · survive waves
description: 3D hack-and-slash prototype. Swipe to cut monsters, chain combos, survive waves. Play instantly in your browser.
features: A mix of Fruit Ninja and music game concepts
Swipe across monsters to slash them
Built with Three.js, runs in mobile browser
designNotes: The development goal is to create lightweight games that can be played immediately after being forwarded on a mobile phone.
Prototype #001 — First in a series of 3D mobile browser games.
The idea started from a simple question: what if Fruit Ninja was 3D and the fruits fought back?

Built the slash mechanic first — felt flat. Added screen shake, and it immediately felt better. Then added combo timing where the window gets tighter the higher your streak. It started to feel like a rhythm game. That wasn't planned, but it works.

devLog.title: Screen shake
devLog.body: A small camera offset on hit — just enough for the body to feel it before the eyes notice. Too much and it's nauseating, too little and it's invisible.

devLog.title: Combo timing
devLog.body: Perfect, Good, and Miss tiers. Faster slashes get stricter windows but higher scores. It gives players a goal beyond just killing — slash with precision.

devLog.title: What's next
devLog.body: There are a few directions being considered for future iterations. Procedural audio is one — having different pitch per combo tier so that chaining Perfects could form a rising scale, letting players "play" their own melody through combat. Another idea is rhythm variety through monster types: mixing fast one-hit enemies with precision-based and timing-critical ones to force constant rhythm switching.

name: Sky Roller
tagline: Ball race · dodge crates · ragdoll physics
description: 7-player ball race on a sky track. Dodge crates, collect buffs, ragdoll physics. Play instantly in your browser.
features: Drag to steer, swipe down to roll faster
GLTF characters with skeletal Run/Death animations
Physics ragdoll when you hit obstacles
designNotes: Prototype #002 — Second in a series of 3D mobile browser games.

Ball size is the core trade-off. Small balls are fast but one crate hit sends you flying. Iron balls are invincible but crawl along. Every race the AI gets a random mix, so the dynamics change each round.
The ragdoll uses cannon-es physics for shattered body parts and a separate flying character that launches forward along the track. Watching 3 racers get wiped out by the same crate never gets old.
Buff pads were the last addition but became the highlight — getting launched into the sky and bouncing twice before landing gives a rollercoaster feeling that pure ground racing doesn't have.

devLog.title: SkinnedMesh clone trap
devLog.body: Three.js Object3D.clone() doesn't copy bone bindings for SkinnedMesh. Characters were stuck in T-pose until switching to SkeletonUtils.clone. A silent bug — no error, just frozen models.

devLog.title: Animation binding
devLog.body: Each character needs its own cloned AnimationClips. Sharing clips between multiple AnimationMixers causes binding conflicts where some characters freeze while others play normally.

devLog.title: What makes it feel right
devLog.body: The game over cooldown — 3 seconds before you can restart. Forces a breath, makes each crash register. And the dust particles behind rolling balls scale with speed, so fast feels fast even before looking at the speedometer.

name: Territory Conquest
tagline: Claim land · close loops · conquer the grid
description: 4-player territory war on a 3D grid. Drag to paint, close loops to claim land. Play instantly in your browser.
features: Drag to move, close loops to claim territory
Roguelike Attack-path upgrades (Blade Rush, Shotgun, Bomber)
Procedural audio — 23 sounds, zero external files
designNotes: Prototype #006 — A paper.io-style territory conquest game.
The core loop is simple: drag your hero to paint tiles, return home to claim enclosed land. But with 4 factions fighting over a 200x200 grid, simple becomes chaotic fast.

The roguelike skill system was originally 3 paths (Attack, Commander, Speed) with 3 tiers each. Simplified to Attack-only for a tighter experience — Blade Rush for melee cleave, Shotgun for ranged spread, Bomber for area denial with fire trails.
All 23 sound effects are procedurally generated via Web Audio API oscillators and noise. No audio files loaded at all — the entire game is a single HTML file with GLTF models.

devLog.title: Flood fill at scale
devLog.body: 200x200 grid means 40,000 cells. Running BFS flood fill every frame would kill performance. Solution: incremental BFS that processes a fixed budget of cells per frame, spreading the work across multiple frames while keeping 60fps.

devLog.title: Influence map AI
devLog.body: Each AI faction evaluates a 50x50 downsampled influence grid to decide where to expand. Factors include enemy density, unclaimed territory value, and distance to home base. This prevents all 3 AI from dogpiling the same corner.

devLog.title: Procedural audio
devLog.body: 23 distinct sounds from pure synthesis: square waves for footsteps, filtered noise for explosions, FM synthesis for level-up chimes. The audio unlock dance for mobile browsers was the trickiest part — multiple event listeners on first touch to satisfy autoplay policies.

name: Wall Wars
tagline: Draw walls · claim territory · real-time multiplayer
description: Real-time multiplayer territory war. Draw walls on a 20x20 grid, enclose areas to claim them. Play with friends or solo vs AI.
features: Create rooms with 4-letter codes, share with friends
designNotes: Prototype #008 — First real-time multiplayer game in the series.

The server runs on Cloudflare Workers with Durable Objects. Each room is a single DO instance that manages game state, tick loop, and WebSocket connections. Room codes use idFromName() so any Worker edge can resolve the same room.
The flood-fill BFS runs server-side every tick to determine territory ownership. When walls fully enclose an area, it flips to the wall owner. Simple mechanic, emergent strategy.
Ink system adds resource management — you can't wall spam. Regen is constant, so knowing when to save ink vs when to rush-build matters.

devLog.title: Durable Object gotcha
devLog.body: Cloudflare free tier requires new_sqlite_classes in wrangler.toml, not new_classes. The error message doesn't mention this — it just says the class isn't found. Cost 2 hours to debug.

devLog.title: WebSocket non-Hibernation
devLog.body: Initially used the Hibernation API (this.state.acceptWebSocket) but it drops in-memory state on idle. Switched to server.accept() for the non-Hibernation path — keeps all player state in RAM for the duration of the game.

devLog.title: Client-side prediction
devLog.body: Wall placement is sent to server AND shown immediately on client. Server state overwrites on next tick. The visual lag is under 100ms so prediction rarely visibly corrects. Makes the game feel instant even on mobile networks.
